title: LoRA: Freezing the Backbone
concept: LoRA freezes the pretrained model weights, adding small, trainable rank decomposition matrices to the attention layers to learn the task updates.

exercises.prompt: Why does LoRA freeze the backbone weights of a pretrained model?

options.text: To keep them unchanged and save memory.

exercises.explain: LoRA freezes the main pre-trained weights to keep them safe and unchanged, avoiding the high cost of updating billions of parameters.

cards.title: Don't Rebuild the Engine
cards.body: Imagine trying to tune up a race car, but instead of rebuilding the entire engine block from scratch, you just tweak a tiny chip in the fuel injector. That is what LoRA does to massive AI models!

cards.title: Freezing the Backbone
cards.body: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) works by freezing the original pretrained model weights so they cannot change. Then, it adds small, trainable pairs of matrices (called rank decomposition matrices) to the attention layers to capture and learn the new task updates.

cards.title: The Sticky Note Encyclopedia
cards.body: Imagine a giant, heavy encyclopedia that is completely glued shut. You cannot erase or rewrite any of its pages. Instead, you stick tiny, erasable sticky notes on the margins to write your new custom notes. The encyclopedia is the frozen backbone, and the sticky notes are the lightweight LoRA matrices!

cards.title: LoRA in Action
cards.body: If you want to train a massive medical chatbot, you don't change its billions of original English language weights. Instead, you freeze them, add small LoRA layers, and train only those small layers on medical questions. This requires way less computer memory!

cards.title: Myth: Frozen Means Unchanging
cards.body: Some people think freezing the backbone means the model cannot learn new things. Actually, the model learns just as well! The small added layers absorb the new task knowledge perfectly without messing up what the model already knew.

cards.title: Full Tuning vs. LoRA
cards.body: Let's look at how weights are handled during training in standard tuning versus LoRA tuning.

left.text: All billions of original weights are unfrozen and updated. Requires massive compute power.

right.text: Original weights are frozen. Only tiny, added adapter matrices are updated. Very fast and light.

exercises.prompt: What happens to the original pretrained weights of a model when you use LoRA?

options.text: They are frozen and do not change.
They are deleted to save storage space.
They are fully retrained from scratch.
They are shuffled randomly before training.

exercises.explain: LoRA stands for Low-Rank Adaptation. It keeps the main backbone frozen and only updates the small adapter layers to save memory and time.

exercises.prompt: True or False: LoRA adds small, trainable rank decomposition matrices to the model's attention layers.
exercises.explain: This is true. LoRA inserts these small, trainable rank decomposition matrices directly into the model's attention layers.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete how LoRA saves computer memory.
exercises.sentence: LoRA keeps the main pretrained weights [blank] while training only the small added matrices.
exercises.explain: By keeping the original weights frozen, we don't have to calculate updates for billions of parameters, which saves a massive amount of computer memory.

exercises.prompt: Which system is using the LoRA method to adapt a pre-trained model?

options.text: System A: It updates every single parameter in the original neural network backbone.
System B: It freezes the original model weights and only trains small helper matrices.
System C: It deletes the original backbone and trains a completely new tiny model.

exercises.explain: System B is correct because LoRA specifically freezes the main backbone weights to save compute resources, rather than retraining everything.

exercises.prompt: A team wants to fine-tune a massive 70-billion parameter model on a budget using LoRA. How does LoRA handle the weights to achieve this?

options.text: It updates only the first half of the original network layers.
It randomly deletes half of the model's parameters to save space.
It freezes the main model weights and trains only the small added matrices.
It retrains all the model parameters using a slower, smaller learning rate.

exercises.explain: LoRA leaves the original model weights completely untouched ('frozen') to protect the general knowledge it already has. It only trains the newly added, tiny adapter matrices to learn the new task.
